edges.evidence: The 2024 paper studies the same core phenomenon of recursive training on generated data causing degradation, extending the earlier 2023 formulation with a broader and more mature treatment.
Both papers analyze self-consuming generative training loops, and the 2024 work extends the earlier MAD framing of collapse under repeated training on synthetic data.
Watermarking addresses the same problem of identifying LLM-generated text but proposes a proactive generation-time solution rather than post hoc detection.

The paraphrasing paper argues that detector-based identification is easily evaded, challenging the possibility of reliably detecting AI-generated text claimed or examined in the detector-reliability paper.
Both papers discuss AI-assisted academic cheating and detector-based responses, and the later responsible-collaboration framing broadens the earlier plagiarism-control discussion.
The 2024 study extends the earlier finding on paraphrasing-based evasion by testing multiple simple detector-bypass techniques across major GenAI detection systems.
Principle-Driven Self-Alignment extends self-instruction ideas by moving from self-generated tasks to principle-guided self-alignment with minimal human supervision.

Both papers address preference-based alignment of language models, and DPO provides a direct optimization framework that is naturally applicable when preference labels come from AI feedback as in RLAIF.
RLAIF operationalizes the idea of replacing some human feedback with AI feedback, directly building on prior work advocating minimal-human-supervision alignment.
The theoretical framework on self-concept degradation builds on concerns about whose values and opinions language models mirror, linking opinion reflection to downstream psychological effects.
Both papers use smartphone sensing to study real-world human behavior, and StudentLife applies the earlier reality-mining paradigm specifically to college student wellbeing and performance.
OpenAssistant Conversations extends the instruction-tuning line by providing a broader open conversational dataset for aligning assistants beyond self-generated single-turn instructions.
LIMA argues that a small amount of high-quality instruction data is sufficient for strong alignment, opposing Self-Instruct's emphasis on large-scale self-generated instruction sets.
WizardLM generates increasingly complex instructions for tuning LLMs, which builds on Self-Instruct's earlier idea of using model-generated instruction data for alignment.
